title: Axon License Plate Reader Contract — Data Ownership, Privacy, and Federal Access Protections
summary: Joint Public Safety and Economic Development committee reviews proposed Axon contract for 26 license plate reader (LPR) cameras. Deputy Chief Trudeau explains LPR functionality: cameras read plates, data owned solely by SPD in a closed system (vendor has no access). LPRs used reactively for investigations, not live monitoring — vehicles involved in approximately 70% of crimes. Key discussion on federal immigration data access: SPD will not share LPR data with ICE or federal agencies absent a court order or subpoena. Chief confirms Axon system is opt-in by default (unlike previous Flock system that inadvertently left data-sharing enabled). Future goal: local law enforcement data-sharing MOAs with Onondaga County agencies, with explicit restrictions preventing further dissemination.

[00:05:21] thought we'd kick it off by going over

[00:05:31] because the terms until we actually sign

[00:05:37] attorney client privilege. Let's just

[00:05:46] uh you know these 26 uh LPR readers um
[00:05:50] for public safety and for

[00:05:54] product does um depending on the uh the

[00:05:58] goes on a fixed pole. Um it uh has a

[00:06:08] various speeds, highway speeds, traffic
[00:06:10] speeds, and then compiles that data into
[00:06:12] a system that allows the police
[00:06:14] department to go back and based on
[00:06:17] criminal investigations
[00:06:20] search that data to assist us in, you
[00:06:24] know, solving crime. So, that's the the
[00:06:28] basic uh premise behind the LPRs and and
[00:06:30] what SPD is looking for uh in their use.
[00:06:34] And and I would like to note because I
[00:06:36] think there are a few points that are um
[00:06:39] vitally [clears throat] important for
[00:06:40] this body and the public to understand.
[00:06:43] Um the data that is collected as a
[00:06:46] result of the installation of LPRs is
[00:06:49] data that is solely owned and accessed
[00:06:52] by SPD in the city. It is not owned or
[00:06:55] controlled by the vendor. they provide a
[00:06:58] clo what's called a closed system so
[00:07:00] that only the data is only being uh
[00:07:04] transmitted to SPD for their use. So I
[00:07:07] think that that is an important feature
[00:07:10] um that may be of import. So they can't
[00:07:14] share data because they don't have data
[00:07:16] to share the data only lives within SPD.
[00:07:21] >> Thanks for sharing that Sue that that is
[00:07:23] a huge concern in our community. So
[00:07:25] thank you. Um, one [clears throat]
[00:07:26] question I do have, Deputy Chief, is um,
[00:07:30] CRI with our crime rates, um, how how
[00:07:34] many crimes do you estimate that we're
[00:07:36] solving because of these cameras?
[00:07:38] >> You took my question.
[00:07:39] >> Oh, I'm so sorry. [laughter] I stole his
[00:07:41] question.
[00:07:42] >> So, percentage-wise, I can't I don't
[00:07:44] have a number percentage- wise. That'd
[00:07:46] be an anecdotal number. But for our
[00:07:48] major crimes, there's almost any no
[00:07:49] homicides at all that don't happen to
[00:07:51] have a vehicle involved in them. So I
[00:07:54] can tell you that for our major crimes
[00:07:56] they're used frequently. I think Axon
[00:07:58] put a number at 70% of crimes overall
[00:08:01] their number is a vehicle is involved in
[00:08:05] the crime. Um so you know for our major
[00:08:07] crimes the percentage is high and I
[00:08:10] think overall that that number is is
[00:08:11] pretty accurate you know based on that.
[00:08:14] In thinking about evidence and closing
[00:08:16] cases,
[00:08:18] I imagine that in order to,
[00:08:22] you know, go to a prosecutor, you have
[00:08:23] to have a number of different points of
[00:08:25] evidence.
[00:08:27] How critical would LPR data be compared
[00:08:30] to all of the other data that you would
[00:08:32] bring to a prosecutor and what does that
[00:08:34] look like?
[00:08:35] >> Yeah. So, I mean, I think I think you
[00:08:37] know there's going to be witness victim
[00:08:40] statements. All right. And we all know
[00:08:41] how how those identifications or witness
[00:08:43] statements can go in terms of that. I
[00:08:46] think this LPR data is factbased, right?
[00:08:50] It takes a review by an officer, right?
[00:08:52] I mean, we're not just going by the
[00:08:54] read. We have to go into the system, but
[00:08:55] we're actually looking at that plate.
[00:08:57] We're looking at that car. We know that
[00:08:59] car was there. And I think what it does
[00:09:02] is it opens us up to additional leads.
[00:09:06] That's what the LPR system does, right?
[00:09:08] I think once we know that car, now we
[00:09:10] have an avenue to get into potential
[00:09:13] other people. Now we can track that car
[00:09:15] to other locations where there may they
[00:09:17] go into a store or there may be other
[00:09:18] evidence or they say they purchase
[00:09:20] something. We're we're looking at, you
[00:09:22] know, those financial records. So I
[00:09:23] think that's very important in
[00:09:28] establishing that first lead that allows
[00:09:30] detectives and officers to build from
[00:09:32] there. But it's not just the lead,
[00:09:35] right? There's a review process that
[00:09:37] takes place based on that that um that
[00:09:40] hit.
[00:09:42] >> Another question I have, and again, this
[00:09:43] is probably just to quell the public's
[00:09:46] concerns.
[00:09:46] >> Sure.
[00:09:47] >> Um you're not staring at these cameras
[00:09:49] looking for crime, right? These you're
[00:09:51] going to the cameras once a crime's been
[00:09:52] committed and you're trying to use them
[00:09:54] to solve it, right?
[00:09:55] >> Yeah, that's a great question. There's
[00:09:56] no active monitoring of the LPR cameras
[00:09:59] like our cops cameras. Um so this is uh
[00:10:02] after the fact. um you know, somebody
[00:10:04] provides a partial plate, somebody
[00:10:06] provides a location where it occurred
[00:10:08] and we're going in and we're
[00:10:09] specifically looking at that time frame.
[00:10:11] We're specifically looking at the
[00:10:13] location where this might have occurred
[00:10:15] and um there's no active monitoring.
[00:10:18] >> Thank you.
[00:10:19] >> So, I think the
[00:10:21] the hot topic, right? What can we talk
[00:10:25] about the legality uh of the data that
[00:10:28] comes from the LPRs? And this might be a
[00:10:30] joint question. If we get a request from
[00:10:34] a federal immigration agency to access
[00:10:37] that data, what that looks like on our
[00:10:39] end if they ask for it?
[00:10:41] >> Yeah. So, I think um this came up in the
[00:10:43] surveillance committee's review. Um and
[00:10:46] absent a court order or a subpoena
[00:10:50] in, you know, from uh ICE or a federal
[00:10:53] entity, we will not share that that
[00:10:56] information.
[00:10:56] >> And that's Sorry, I'm stealing your
[00:10:58] phone. Go ahead. That subpoena goes to
[00:11:00] who, Sue? If they were to subpoena the
[00:11:02] data looking for person. [clears throat]
[00:11:05] >> So because the data is owned and housed
[00:11:08] only with SPD,
[00:11:12] I can't tell you that the vendor
[00:11:14] wouldn't receive a subpoena, but they
[00:11:17] don't have the information to supply
[00:11:19] back. So they would let us know that
[00:11:21] they received a subpoena, but only SPD
[00:11:24] has data that would be responsive to
[00:11:26] that subpoena. So it would have to come
[00:11:28] through this office in order to be
[00:11:31] responsive to that subpoena. Does that
[00:11:33] answer your question?
[00:11:35] >> And that's the only way we'll be would
[00:11:36] be sharing.
[00:11:38] >> Correct.
[00:11:39] >> Have no other choice. I have
[00:11:41] >> correct for the federal entities. That's
[00:11:43] correct. Y
[00:11:44] >> and chief now let's you know take the
[00:11:46] federal government out of the equation.
[00:11:48] What if we're talking about dit or
[00:11:49] manest or New York state? What what does
[00:11:52] that process look like? So right now
[00:11:54] that our current footprint is that we're
[00:11:55] not sharing information with uh those
[00:11:58] other agencies. We would like to enter
[00:12:00] into MOAS down the line that says we'll
[00:12:04] share with you our data but you are not
[00:12:06] allowed to share that data beyond just
[00:12:09] yourself. I think there is value in the
[00:12:12] collective law enforcement community
[00:12:14] within Anadaga County cuz crime doesn't
[00:12:17] stop at our borders um to share that
[00:12:19] information locally for criminal
[00:12:22] prosecutions.
[00:12:24] But that agreement which we still have
[00:12:26] to work out would be just for that
[00:12:28] agency that we're working with. There's
[00:12:30] no sharing of that information beyond
[00:12:33] that entity to ICE or to other agencies.
[00:12:37] And I think what you're saying, chief,
[00:12:39] is the request would have to be
[00:12:41] specific, tied to a crime at on a date
[00:12:44] at an area. It's not, hey, we want to
[00:12:46] see your LPR data for the month of
[00:12:48] January. That's not what chief is
[00:12:51] talking about. It would be tied to a
[00:12:52] specific crime that had occurred and the
[00:12:56] adjacent law enforcement agency locally
[00:12:58] is trying to solve that incident
[00:13:02] >> and with so along the same road. So with
[00:13:05] in the past with previous
[00:13:08] surveyors, we had for a year or so
[00:13:11] accidentally or not accidentally left a
[00:13:14] button on on that website that was
[00:13:15] sharing our data with other agencies
[00:13:17] across the country. Can we be assured
[00:13:20] that with this new one, [snorts] none of
[00:13:22] those buttons are on?
[00:13:23] >> Yeah, that's absolutely the case. This
[00:13:24] is an optin situation, not an opt out.
[00:13:27] So as a standard, you're not sharing
[00:13:30] with anyone unless you check the box to
[00:13:32] do that. So we are assuring you that
[00:13:35] we're not sharing any of that data with
[00:13:37] the folks that or the entities that
[00:13:38] you're concerned with. And I just want a
[00:13:40] point of clarification.
[00:13:42] It for local agencies our footprint
[00:13:46] would be if we are able to share that
[00:13:49] data so that they can come into their
[00:13:52] systems and look at our data on an
[00:13:53] ongoing basis if that's not something
[00:13:55] that we want to do. But that would be
[00:13:56] our position because I I can assure you
[00:13:59] that with Dwit, the sheriff's
[00:14:01] department, um you know, towns and
[00:14:04] villages, again, crime is so transient
[00:14:06] between our small community that if
[00:14:09] able, we would like to share that data,
[00:14:10] but to have safeguards in place in an
[00:14:14] MOA that says you guys can't share it
[00:14:17] with somebody else. That would be where
[00:14:18] we'd like to go, but that is not where
[00:14:20] we're at now. And there'll be a lot of
[00:14:21] discussions to get there. I just wanted
[00:14:23] to clarify that. no sharing with federal
[00:14:25] agencies at all. But Anadaga County as a
[00:14:28] community, I think, benefits from a
[00:14:31] sharing to some degree of law
[00:14:33] enforcement data that's got guard rails
